perturbation.original_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence, and will exercise at least the same care with respect thereto as it exercises with respect to its’ own confidential or proprietary information and will not without Hydranautics’ consent use, divulge, co py, release, sell, loan or otherwise reveal such Confidential Information to others.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor agrees to make reasonable efforts to hold the Confidential Information in confidence, and will exercise care with respect thereto, but is not strictly prohibited from using, divulging, copying, releasing, selling, loaning or otherwise revealing such Confidential Information to others if deemed necessary by the Visitor.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the visitor to hold the confidential information in the strictest confidence, while the modified text reduces the commitment to 'reasonable efforts,' introduces subjective judgment ('if deemed necessary by the Visitor'), and weakens the prohibition on disclosure, creating ambiguity around the visitor's obligations. This undermines the intended confidentiality of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA provides for injunctive relief and damages for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to hold confidential information in the strictest confidence and allowing for disclosure 'if deemed necessary by the Visitor,' the modified text contradicts the intent of CUTSA, making it more difficult for Hydranautics to pursue legal action if a visitor misappropriates or discloses confidential information, as the visitor could argue that their actions were deemed 'necessary.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets as outlined in CUTSA. The original text mandates the 'strictest confidence,' while the changed text reduces this to 'reasonable efforts' and allows disclosure if 'deemed necessary by the Visitor.' This directly contradicts the duty to maintain secrecy, a breach of which is considered 'improper means' of misappropriation according to California Civil Code 3426.1(a). By allowing the visitor to subjectively decide when disclosure is 'necessary,' the contract weakens the protection against misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor will abide by all safety regulations, stay within designated areas of the facility per the established tour, and will not leave the touring party without an escort by a Hydranautics authorized employee.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor will generally try to abide by safety suggestions, mostly stay within suggested areas of the facility, and may occasionally leave the touring party without an escort.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates adherence to safety regulations, staying within designated areas, and remaining with the tour group. The modified text uses softer language ('generally try,' 'mostly stay,' 'may occasionally leave'), weakening these obligations and making them discretionary, which compromises safety protocols.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Regulations, Title 8, Section 3203
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 CCR § 3203 requires employers to establish, implement, and maintain an effective Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP), which includes workplace safety rules. By suggesting that visitors only 'generally try' to abide by safety regulations and 'mostly stay' within designated areas, the modified text undermines Hydranautics' ability to ensure a safe environment and contradicts the spirit and intent of the IIPP regulations, potentially exposing Hydranautics to liability if a visitor is injured due to non-compliance with safety rules.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code of Regulations, Title 8, Section 3203. Injury and Illness Prevention Program. Skip to Main Content This information is provided free of charge by the Department of Industrial Relations from its web site at www.dir.ca.gov . These regulations are for the convenience of the user and no representation or warranty is made that the information is current or accurate. See full disclaimer at https://www.dir.ca.gov/od_pub/disclaimer.html . Subchapter 7. General Industry Safety Orders Group 1. General Physical Conditions and Structures Orders Introduction Return to index New query §3203. Injury and Illness Prevention Program. (a) Effective July 1, 1991, every employer shall establish, implement and maintain an effective Injury and Illness Prevention Program (Program). The Program shall be in writing and, shall, at a minimum: (1) Identify the person or persons with authority and responsibility for implementing the Program. (2) Include a system for ensuring that employees comply with safe and healthy work practices. Substantial compliance with this provision includes recognition of employees who follow safe and healthful work practices, training and retraining programs, disciplinary actions, or any other such means that ensures employee compliance with safe and healthful work practices. (3) Include a system for communicating with employees in a form readily understandable by all affected employees on matters relating to occupational safety and health, including provisions designed to encourage employees to inform the employer of hazards at the worksite without fear of reprisal. Substantial compliance with this provision includes meetings, training programs, posting, written communications, a system of anonymous notification by employees about hazards, labor/management safety and health committees, or any other means that ensures communication with employees. Exception: Employers having fewer than 10 employees shall be permitted to communicate to and instruct employees orally in general safe work practices with spe
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - California Code of Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The modified text uses permissive language ('generally try,' 'mostly stay,' 'may occasionally leave') which weakens the original mandatory obligations concerning safety. This directly contradicts the Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP), as outlined in 8 CCR § 3203, which requires employers to 'establish, implement and maintain an effective Injury and Illness Prevention Program (Program)'. The altered contract language compromises the employer's ability to ensure visitor compliance with safety regulations, thus undermining the core intent of the IIPP to maintain a safe working environment, as mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By weakening the obligation to follow safety regulations, Hydranautics' ability to enforce workplace safety rules is compromised, increasing the risk of accidents or injuries. This contradicts the requirement to implement and maintain an effective safety program.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor agrees he/she will not remove any document, equipment or other materials from the premises without Hydranautics’ written consent. Visitor will not photograph, vid eotape, or otherwise make any record of or preserve any Confidential Information to which he/she may be given access during the facility tour(s).
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor will endeavor not to remove any document, equipment or other materials, and will generally avoid photographing, videotaping or recording Confidential Information unless for personal use.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly prohibits removing materials and recording confidential information. The modified text softens the prohibition on removing items to 'endeavor not to,' and allows for photographing, videotaping, or recording confidential information 'for personal use,' which is a vague exception that undermines the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing visitors to photograph, videotape, or record confidential information 'for personal use,' the modified text creates a loophole that could enable misappropriation and make it difficult for Hydranautics to prove that a visitor's actions violated the agreement. The ambiguity surrounding 'personal use' directly contradicts the intent of CUTSA to protect trade secrets from unauthorized use and disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the protection of confidential information, creating a loophole that could enable misappropriation. The original text strictly prohibited removing materials and recording confidential information. The changed text softens the prohibition on removing items and allows for photographing, videotaping, or recording confidential information 'for personal use.' This directly contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets from unauthorized use and disclosure, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supports the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. The scraped law snippets, while not directly stating the prohibition, cite the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which aims to protect trade secrets; the modified text undermines this protection by allowing a vague 'personal use' exception.

perturbation.original_text: The term “Confidential Information” shall include all Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that conc erns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other proprietary business information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Confidential Information” may generally include Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that arguably concerns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto, potentially including financial information, business plans, etc., but not necessarily.
perturbation.explanation: By using the terms 'may generally include,' 'arguably concerns,' and 'potentially including, but not necessarily,' the modified text introduces significant ambiguity into the definition of Confidential Information. This weakens the scope of what is protected and makes it more difficult to enforce the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, the plaintiff must be able to clearly define what constitutes a 'trade secret' in order to successfully prosecute a misappropriation claim. The modified text's vague definition of 'Confidential Information' undermines the clarity and specificity required to establish a trade secret under CUTSA, thus hindering Hydranautics' ability to enforce its rights if a visitor misappropriates information. A clear and specific definition is necessary to prove that information was indeed a trade secret and that the defendant misappropriated it.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT - Sections 3426-3426.11 :: California Civil Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Civil Code Sections 3426-3426.11 TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT CIVIL CODE SECTION 3426-3426.11 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may b
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into the definition of 'Confidential Information,' which undermines the specificity required for trade secret protection under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines a trade secret as information that '(1) Derives independent economic value...from not being generally known...; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable...to maintain its secrecy.' By using terms like 'may generally include,' 'arguably concerns,' and 'potentially including, but not necessarily,' the modified text weakens the efforts to maintain secrecy and makes it more difficult to prove that the information derives independent economic value from being secret, creating a legal contradiction because enforceability is significantly undermined.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor understands that his or her breach of this Agreement may subject him or her and his or her employees to liability.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor vaguely understands that a perceived breach of this Agreement might, in certain circumstances, subject him or her and his or her employees to potential liability.
perturbation.explanation: The original statement informs the visitor that breaching the agreement may lead to liability. The modified text weakens this warning by adding qualifiers like 'vaguely understands,' 'perceived breach,' 'might,' 'certain circumstances,' and 'potential liability,' making the consequences of a breach uncertain and diminishing the deterrent effect of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1550 outlines the essential elements of a contract, including a clear understanding of the obligations and consequences of breach by all parties involved. By weakening the clarity of understanding regarding breach consequences, the modified text reduces the likelihood that a court would find the contract to be based on a mutual understanding of the terms, potentially rendering the breach and liability clause unenforceable. The more ambiguous the understanding, the less certain that there was a 'meeting of the minds' necessary for the contract to be valid.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Definition [1549 - 1550.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 1550. It is essential to the existence of a contract that there should be: 1. Parties capable of contracting; 2. Their consent; 3. A lawful object; and, 4. A sufficient cause or consideration. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the visitor's understanding of potential liability for breaching the agreement, contradicting the requirement for clear consent and understanding of contractual obligations as stated in California Civil Code § 1550. The original text states "Visitor understands that his or her breach of this Agreement may subject him or her and his or her employees to liability". The modified text weakens this to "Visitor vaguely understands that a perceived breach of this Agreement might, in certain circumstances, subject him or her and his or her employees to potential liability". This ambiguity undermines the mutual understanding necessary for a valid contract, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state: "It is essential to the existence of a contract that there should be: 1. Parties capable of contracting; 2. Their consent". By weakening the clarity of understanding, the 'consent' element is less certain, creating a contradiction. The added qualifiers diminish the deterrent effect and create a scenario where the consequences of a breach are uncertain, directly contradicting the need for clear understanding of the contractual terms.
